title: Business Statistics
text:
Welcome
This course introduces the statistical foundations essential for modern business decision-making. Statistics has become the backbone of evidence-based management, marketing analytics, financial modelling, and artificial intelligence. Whether you work in consulting, finance, HR, or tech, the ability to reason from data is a core professional skill.
The course covers both conceptual understanding and practical implementation — primarily in Python and R — so that you can apply the methods immediately in real-world contexts.
crumbs: 1  Business Statistics — From Data to Decisions

title: Business Statistics
text:
Who Is This Course For?
This course is designed for students of business administration, economics, and social sciences who want to build a solid foundation in applied statistics. No advanced mathematics is required, but comfort with basic algebra is helpful.
crumbs: 1  Business Statistics — From Data to Decisions

Course Structure

1. Foundations
Why statistics matters; variables and measurement; intro to R and Markdown

Full SEM, path diagrams with semPlot, mediation, measurement invariance

title: 1.1. Statistics in Business
text:
1.1.1. Why Does Business Need Statistics?
Modern businesses generate data at an unprecedented scale — from transaction logs and customer behaviour to supply chain events and social media signals. Statistics provides the tools to extract actionable knowledge from this data. Without a statistical foundation, decisions are based on intuition alone, which is often biased and unreliable.
Key business functions that rely on statistics:







Function
Statistical application




Marketing
A/B testing, customer segmentation, churn prediction


Finance
Risk modelling, portfolio optimisation, fraud detection


HR
Performance evaluation, turnover analysis, pay equity


Operations
Quality control, demand forecasting, process optimisation


Strategy
Market research, scenario analysis, KPI benchmarking





1.1.2. Datafication of the World
Datafication refers to the transformation of social actions and everyday behaviours into quantifiable data. Virtually every human activity — shopping, commuting, communicating — now leaves a digital trace. This creates both opportunities (insights from behaviour patterns) and risks (privacy, algorithmic bias, surveillance).
Statisticians and data scientists are among the fastest-growing professions globally. According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, employment in data-related occupations is projected to grow 35% between 2022 and 2032 — far above the average for all occupations.


1.1.3. Statistical Thinking vs. Statistical Manipulation
Statistics is a powerful tool, but it can be misused. Common sources of statistical manipulation:

Bad sampling — surveying only people who agree with you (self-selection bias)
Bad processing — cherry-picking time windows or subgroups that support a preferred conclusion
Bad interpretation — confusing correlation with causation, or ignoring effect size when p < 0.05







WarningThe p-value controversy



For decades, the threshold p < 0.05 was treated as a binary gate to “significance.” A landmark 2019 commentary in Nature signed by more than 800 scientists argued that this threshold should be retired in favour of reporting effect sizes, confidence intervals, and the full context of the finding (Black 2009).




1.1.4. Inductive vs. Deductive Reasoning
Statistical reasoning is fundamentally inductive: we observe a sample and draw conclusions about a population. This is the opposite of deductive reasoning, which starts from general principles and derives specific conclusions.

Deduction: All humans are mortal → Socrates is human → Socrates is mortal.
Induction: We measured 200 customers, 68% preferred product A → probably most customers prefer product A.

Both approaches have their place, but empirical social science and business analytics are built on induction — and statistics gives it a rigorous formal framework.


1.1.5. The Statistics–AI Connection
Artificial intelligence, and machine learning in particular, is applied statistics at scale. Regression, Bayesian inference, matrix decomposition, and probabilistic graphical models are foundational to every major AI system. Understanding statistics is therefore not just useful for data analysis — it is essential for evaluating, deploying, and critically assessing AI tools in a business context.
Key statistical concepts used directly in AI/ML:

Probability distributions — the building blocks of generative models
Maximum likelihood estimation — the workhorse of model training
Bias–variance tradeoff — the central challenge in generalisation
Cross-validation — the standard approach to model evaluation
Regularisation — the statistical answer to overfitting



1.1.6. How to Learn Statistics Effectively
Statistics is best learned by doing. Passive reading is insufficient; you need to work through problems, implement methods in code, and critically examine real datasets.
Recommended learning path:

Build intuition through worked examples and visualisations
Derive key formulas manually at least once — understanding the mechanics prevents misuse
Implement every method in Python or R
Apply to a dataset from your own domain — the closer the data to your interests, the more motivated you will be
Read critically — examine how statistics is used (and misused) in news, research papers, and business reports





Black, Ken. 2009. Business Statistics: Contemporary Decision Making. John Wiley & Sons.

title: 1.2. Variables and Measurement Scales
text:
1.2.1. What Is a Variable?
A variable is any characteristic, number, or quantity that can be measured or counted. It is called a variable because its value may differ between observations (data units) and may change over time.
Examples of variables in business: - Customer age, annual income, satisfaction score - Product category, country of origin, payment method - Number of units sold, daily revenue, stock return


1.2.2. Types of Variables
Variables are broadly divided into two families:
Numeric (quantitative) variables represent measurable quantities:

Discrete — countable values with no meaningful intermediate (number of employees, number of defects)
Continuous — any value within a range (revenue, temperature, time)

Categorical (qualitative) variables represent groups or categories:

Nominal — categories with no inherent order (country, product type, gender)
Ordinal — categories with a meaningful order but undefined spacing (satisfaction: low / medium / high)



1.2.3. Levels of Measurement
The level of measurement (measurement scale) determines which statistical operations are valid for a variable. Stevens (1946) proposed four levels, still used universally:



Scale
Equality
Order
Distances
Absolute zero
Example




Nominal
✓
✗
✗
✗
Eye colour, nationality


Ordinal
✓
✓
✗
✗
Education level, Likert rating


Interval
✓
✓
✓
✗
Temperature (°C), calendar year


Ratio
✓
✓
✓
✓
Income, weight, age



Why does this matter? The scale of a variable determines the permissible statistics:

Nominal: frequency counts, mode, chi-square tests
Ordinal: median, percentiles, Spearman correlation
Interval/Ratio: mean, standard deviation, Pearson correlation, regression







Note



A common mistake is calculating means for ordinal data (e.g., averaging a 1–5 satisfaction scale). This is technically invalid because the distances between scale points are undefined, but it is widely done in practice and the debate continues in methodology literature.




1.2.4. Independent and Dependent Variables
In research and modelling, variables are classified by their role:

Dependent variable (DV) — the outcome we want to explain or predict. Also called response, outcome, or target variable.
Independent variable (IV) — the variable(s) used to explain variation in the DV. Also called predictor, explanatory variable, or feature.

Example in regression notation (R):
# Predicting happiness from age, income, and gender
model <- lm(Happiness ~ Age + Income + Gender, data = survey)
In this model: - Happiness is the dependent variable (Y) - Age, Income, Gender are independent variables (X₁, X₂, X₃)


1.2.5. Variable Identification in Python
import pandas as pd

# Sample business dataset
data = pd.DataFrame({
    "customer_id": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    "age": [34, 28, 45, 52, 23],
    "income": [52000, 38000, 91000, 67000, 29000],
    "satisfaction": ["high", "medium", "high", "low", "medium"],  # ordinal
    "region": ["North", "South", "South", "East", "West"],        # nominal
    "purchases": [3, 1, 7, 4, 2]                                  # ratio
})

# Check data types
print(data.dtypes)

# Convert ordinal variable to ordered category
data["satisfaction"] = pd.Categorical(
    data["satisfaction"],
    categories=["low", "medium", "high"],
    ordered=True
)
print(data["satisfaction"].cat.codes)  # numeric encoding: low=0, medium=1, high=2


1.2.6. Dependent and Independent Variables in Practice
Identifying the DV and IVs correctly before analysis avoids fundamental logical errors:








Research question
DV
IVs




Does advertising spend predict sales?
Sales
Ad spend


What factors explain employee turnover?
Turnover (yes/no)
Salary, tenure, manager rating


Does customer age affect purchase frequency?
Purchases per month
Age


Is satisfaction different across regions?
Satisfaction score
Region



In causal inference, we additionally need to distinguish correlation from causation — which requires experimental design or careful controls for confounding variables.

title: 1.3. Introduction to R and Markdown
text:
1.3.1. Why R?
R is a free, open-source programming language designed specifically for statistical computing and graphics. It has become the standard tool in academic research, data science, and business analytics because:

It has the richest ecosystem of statistical packages in the world (20 000+ on CRAN)
Publication-quality plots with ggplot2
Reproducible reports with R Markdown and Quarto
Tight integration with Excel, SQL, Python, and cloud platforms
Large, friendly community and extensive free learning resources

R is not a general-purpose language like Python — it is purpose-built for data analysis, which makes it extremely efficient for statistical work.



1.3.2. Installing R and RStudio
Step 1 — Install R
Download R from the official CRAN repository:

Windows/macOS/Linux: https://cran.r-project.org/

Always install the latest release (≥ 4.3.x as of 2024).
Step 2 — Install RStudio Desktop
RStudio is the most popular IDE for R. Download the free version:

https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/

RStudio gives you a script editor, console, environment browser, and plot viewer in one window.
RStudio layout:







Panel
Purpose




Source (top-left)
Write and save R scripts / R Markdown files


Console (bottom-left)
Run commands interactively


Environment (top-right)
See objects in memory


Files / Plots / Help (bottom-right)
Browse files, see plots, read documentation






1.3.3. R Basics

Running code
# This is a comment — R ignores everything after #
1 + 1          # arithmetic
sqrt(144)      # function call
"Hello world"  # string


Variables and assignment
x <- 5         # preferred assignment operator
y = 10         # also works, but <- is conventional in R
name <- "Anna"

cat("x =", x, "\n")  # print to console


Data types
# Numeric
revenue <- 125000.50

# Integer
n_employees <- 45L    # L suffix makes it integer

# Logical
is_profitable <- TRUE

# Character (string)
department <- "Marketing"

# Check type
class(revenue)          # "numeric"
class(is_profitable)    # "logical"


Vectors — the fundamental data structure
# Create a vector with c() — "combine"
scores  <- c(72, 85, 91, 68, 77)
labels  <- c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4", "Q1")

# Basic operations apply element-wise
scores * 1.1            # scale all values by 1.1
scores[scores > 80]     # filter: values above 80

# Summary functions
mean(scores)
median(scores)
sd(scores)
summary(scores)


Data frames — tabular data
df <- data.frame(
  name    = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol"),
  age     = c(28, 34, 29),
  salary  = c(55000, 72000, 61000),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Access columns
df$age
df[["salary"]]

# Filter rows (base R)
df[df$age < 30, ]

# With dplyr (recommended)
library(dplyr)
df |>
  filter(age < 30) |>
  select(name, salary)




1.3.4. Essential Packages
Install packages once; load them every session:
# Install (run once)
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "ggplot2", "dplyr", "readr",
                   "psych", "lavaan", "corrplot", "skimr"))

# Load (run every session)
library(tidyverse)   # data wrangling + ggplot2
library(psych)       # psychological/factor analysis tools
library(lavaan)      # SEM and CFA
library(corrplot)    # correlation matrix plots
library(skimr)       # quick data summaries
The tidyverse is a coherent collection of packages (ggplot2, dplyr, tidyr, readr, purrr, tibble, stringr, forcats) that share a consistent design philosophy.



1.3.5. Loading and Exploring Data
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(skimr)

# Load CSV
df <- read_csv("data/employees.csv")

# Quick overview
glimpse(df)    # column names, types, first values
skim(df)       # rich summary: missing %, distributions

# Base R equivalents
str(df)
summary(df)
head(df, 10)
dim(df)        # rows, columns
names(df)      # column names

Reading Excel files
library(readxl)
df <- read_excel("data/sales_report.xlsx", sheet = "2023")




1.3.6. R Markdown — Reproducible Reports
R Markdown is a file format (.Rmd) that combines narrative text (Markdown), executable R code, and output (tables, plots) into a single document. You render it into HTML, PDF, or Word with one click.

Document structure
---
title: "Sales Analysis Q1 2024"
author: "Kamil Filipek"
date: "2026-04-28"
output: html_document
---

## Overview

This report analyses Q1 sales data.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df <- read_csv("sales_q1.csv")
summary(df)
```
:::


## Revenue by Region


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(total = sum(revenue)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(region, -total), y = total, fill = region)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Revenue by Region", x = NULL, y = "Revenue (€)") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::
Render to HTML: Click Knit in RStudio, or run:
rmarkdown::render("report.Rmd")


Key chunk options



Option
Values
Effect




echo
TRUE/FALSE
Show or hide the code


eval
TRUE/FALSE
Run or skip the code


warning
TRUE/FALSE
Show or hide warnings


message
TRUE/FALSE
Show or hide messages


fig.width, fig.height
numeric
Figure dimensions in inches


include
TRUE/FALSE
Include chunk output at all







1.3.7. Quarto — the Next Generation
Quarto (.qmd) is the successor to R Markdown, developed by Posit. It supports R, Python, Julia, and Observable JS in the same document. This course is written in Quarto.
Key differences from R Markdown:

Uses YAML chunk options inside #| comments
Multi-language in a single document
Built-in support for books, websites, presentations, and interactive dashboards
quarto render document.qmd from the terminal


library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Car weight vs fuel efficiency",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)", y = "Miles per gallon",
       colour = "Cylinders") +
  theme_minimal()

1.3.9. Markdown Syntax Reference
Markdown is a lightweight markup language. Both R Markdown and Quarto use it for text formatting.

Write your analysis in a .qmd or .Rmd file from the very first line. This habit ensures your work is always reproducible and easy to share. Never copy-paste results manually — let the document render them automatically.

title: 2.1. Central Tendency
text:
Descriptive statistics summarise a dataset using a small number of values that capture its essential characteristics. They fall into two groups: measures of central tendency (where the data is centred) and measures of variability (how spread out the data is).

2.1.1. Mean
The arithmetic mean is the sum of all values divided by the number of observations. It is the most commonly used measure of central tendency.
\[\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}\]
data = [1, 4, 5, 6, 8, 3, 5]
mean = sum(data) / len(data)
print(f"Mean: {mean}")        # 4.57

import statistics
print(statistics.mean(data))  # 4.571428...
Weighted arithmetic mean is used when observations contribute unequally to the result (e.g., grades with different weights):
\[\bar{X}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}\]
# Student grade example
grades  = [4, 3, 5]   # exam, activity, homework
weights = [5, 1, 3]   # weights

weighted_mean = sum(g * w for g, w in zip(grades, weights)) / sum(weights)
print(f"Final grade: {weighted_mean:.2f}")   # 4.22
When to use: Interval and ratio data, symmetric distributions without extreme outliers.


2.1.2. Median
The median is the middle value when observations are sorted. It divides the distribution into two equal halves.

Odd number of observations: median = the middle value
Even number of observations: median = average of the two middle values

\[M_{\text{odd}} = x_{\frac{n+1}{2}} \qquad M_{\text{even}} = \frac{x_{\frac{n}{2}} + x_{\frac{n}{2}+1}}{2}\]
import statistics

data_odd  = sorted([3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12])
data_even = sorted([3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35])

print(statistics.median(data_odd))   # 7
print(statistics.median(data_even))  # 8.0  → (7+9)/2
When to use: Ordinal data, or interval/ratio data with skewed distributions or outliers (income, house prices, reaction times).


2.1.3. Mode
The mode is the most frequently occurring value. A dataset can be:

Unimodal — one mode
Bimodal / multimodal — two or more modes
No mode — all values occur equally often

import pandas as pd

data = [3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35, 3, 5, 6, 2, 2, 3, 9, 5, 8, 8, 21, 3]

s = pd.Series(data)
print(s.mode())                 # 3 (most frequent value)
print(s.value_counts().head())  # full frequency table
When to use: Nominal and ordinal data; also useful for continuous data when binned into categories.


2.1.4. Choosing the Right Measure



Situation
Best measure




Symmetric distribution, no extreme values
Mean


Skewed distribution or outliers present
Median


Nominal (categorical) data
Mode


Reporting salary / income / house prices
Median


Grading with different credit weights
Weighted mean









NoteMean vs. Median in practice



Consider household income in a country. The mean is pulled up by a small number of very wealthy households, making it an overestimate of the “typical” income. The median is resistant to these extremes and better represents the middle of the distribution. This is why government reports on income typically report both.




2.1.5. Full Example in Python
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Simulated monthly sales data (€ thousands)
sales = pd.Series([45, 52, 48, 61, 200, 55, 49, 53, 47, 50, 58, 44])

print(f"Mean:   {sales.mean():.1f}")
print(f"Median: {sales.median():.1f}")
print(f"Mode:   {sales.mode().values}")

# Visualise
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
sales.hist(bins=10, ax=ax, color="steelblue", edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(sales.mean(),   color="red",    linestyle="--", label="Mean")
ax.axvline(sales.median(), color="orange", linestyle="-",  label="Median")
ax.set_xlabel("Monthly sales (€k)")
ax.set_title("Effect of an outlier on Mean vs. Median")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
The single outlier (€200k) raises the mean significantly while the median stays close to the cluster of typical months — a clear illustration of why median is preferred for skewed data.

title: 2.2. Variability and Spread
text:
Measures of central tendency tell us where data is centred, but two datasets can have identical means and very different distributions. Measures of variability describe how spread out the values are.

2.2.1. Range
The simplest measure — the difference between the maximum and minimum value:
\[\text{Range} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}\]
data = (3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35, 3, 5, 6, 2, 2, 3, 9, 5, 8, 8, 21, 3)
print(f"Min: {min(data)}, Max: {max(data)}, Range: {max(data) - min(data)}")
# Min: 2, Max: 35, Range: 33
Limitation: extremely sensitive to outliers; tells nothing about the distribution in between.


2.2.2. Variance
Variance measures the average squared deviation from the mean.
Population variance: \[\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}{N}\]
Sample variance (Bessel’s correction — dividing by \(N-1\) makes it an unbiased estimator): \[S^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{X})^2}{N - 1}\]
import statistics

data = [3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35]
print(f"Sample variance: {statistics.variance(data):.2f}")

import numpy as np
print(f"Population variance: {np.var(data):.2f}")
print(f"Sample variance:     {np.var(data, ddof=1):.2f}")


2.2.3. Standard Deviation
Standard deviation is the square root of variance, bringing the measure back to the original units:
\[\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2} \qquad S = \sqrt{S^2}\]
import numpy as np

data = [3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35]
print(f"Standard deviation: {np.std(data, ddof=1):.2f}")
Interpretation rule of thumb (normal distribution): - ~68% of values fall within ±1 SD of the mean - ~95% within ±2 SD - ~99.7% within ±3 SD


2.2.4. Coefficient of Variation
Standard deviation depends on the scale of the variable — it is not directly comparable across datasets measured in different units. The coefficient of variation (CV) normalises SD by the mean:
\[CV = \frac{S}{\bar{X}} \times 100\%\]
import numpy as np

prices_A = [100, 102, 98, 105, 97]   # Product A (stable)
prices_B = [20,  35,  15, 40,  10]   # Product B (volatile)

for name, series in [("A", prices_A), ("B", prices_B)]:
    cv = np.std(series, ddof=1) / np.mean(series) * 100
    print(f"Product {name} — SD: {np.std(series, ddof=1):.2f}, CV: {cv:.1f}%")


2.2.5. Skewness
Skewness measures the asymmetry of a distribution.
\[\text{Skewness} = \frac{3(\bar{X} - M_{\text{median}})}{S}\]

Positive skew (right-skewed): long tail to the right; mean > median. Common in income, wealth, wait times.
Negative skew (left-skewed): long tail to the left; mean < median.
Zero skew: symmetric distribution (e.g., normal distribution).

from scipy.stats import skew
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 5, 8, 15, 30]
print(f"Skewness: {skew(data):.3f}")

plt.hist(data, bins=8, color="steelblue", edgecolor="white")
plt.title(f"Right-skewed distribution (skewness = {skew(data):.2f})")
plt.xlabel("Value")
plt.show()


2.2.6. Kurtosis
Kurtosis describes the “weight” or thickness of the tails relative to a normal distribution.
\[\text{Kurtosis} = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{X})^4}{\sigma^4 \cdot N}\]



Type
Kurtosis value
Tail weight




Mesokurtic (normal)
≈ 3
Normal tails


Leptokurtic
> 3
Fat tails (extreme values more likely)


Platykurtic
< 3
Thin tails (extreme values less likely)



In finance, fat tails are crucial: the Black-Scholes model assumes normal returns, but real markets are leptokurtic — extreme events (“black swans”) are far more common than the model predicts.
from scipy.stats import kurtosis

data = (3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35, 3, 5, 6, 2, 2, 3, 9, 5, 8, 8, 21, 3)
print(f"Kurtosis (excess): {kurtosis(data):.3f}")
# scipy returns excess kurtosis (normal = 0); add 3 to get the raw value


2.2.7. Summary Statistics in One Line
import pandas as pd

data = pd.Series([3, 2, 5, 18, 11, 6, 7, 9, 12, 35, 3, 5, 6, 2, 2, 3, 9, 5, 8, 8, 21, 3])

print(data.describe())
# count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50%, 75%, max
The describe() output gives a five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max) plus mean and standard deviation — the foundation of any exploratory data analysis.


2.2.8. Box Plot — Visualising Variability
A box plot (box-and-whisker plot) displays the five-number summary graphically and highlights outliers.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Monthly revenue for three product lines (€k)
data = {
    "Electronics":  [82, 91, 88, 79, 200, 85, 90, 93],
    "Clothing":     [45, 48, 50, 47, 43,  46, 49, 51],
    "Food":         [30, 31, 29, 35, 28,  33, 32, 30],
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

df.boxplot(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.ylabel("Revenue (€k)")
plt.title("Revenue distribution by product line")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 3.1. Data Quality and Missing Data
text:
5.1.1. Why Data Quality Matters
Before any statistical analysis, the data must be understood, cleaned, and validated. Poor data quality is the leading cause of incorrect analyses and poor business decisions — a fact captured by the phrase “garbage in, garbage out.”
Common data quality problems:








Problem
Description
Impact




Missing values
Observations where one or more variables have no value
Biased estimates, reduced sample size


Outliers
Extreme values that may be errors or genuine extremes
Distorted means, inflated variance


Duplicates
Same observation recorded more than once
Inflated counts, biased models


Encoding errors
Typos, inconsistent categories (“male”, “Male”, “M”)
Wrong groupings, model failures


Wrong data type
Numeric stored as text, dates as strings
Failed computations


Imbalanced classes
One class far more frequent (e.g., fraud: 0.1% of transactions)
Biased classifiers





5.1.2. Mechanisms of Missingness
Understanding why data is missing determines the correct treatment:









Mechanism
Definition
Example
Implication




MCAR (Missing Completely At Random)
Missingness is independent of observed and unobserved data
Random equipment failure
Any imputation is unbiased


MAR (Missing At Random)
Missingness depends on observed data, not on the missing value itself
Older respondents skip income question
Imputation using observed predictors is valid


MNAR (Missing Not At Random)
Missingness depends on the missing value itself
High earners skip income question
Requires special models; simple imputation is biased





5.1.3. Exploratory Checks in Python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
n = 300
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "age":       np.random.randint(18, 65, n),
    "income":    np.random.normal(45000, 15000, n),
    "education": np.random.choice(["Primary", "Secondary", "University", None], n,
                                  p=[0.1, 0.35, 0.45, 0.10]),
    "score":     np.random.normal(70, 12, n),
})
# Introduce MNAR: high-income respondents hide income
df.loc[df["income"] > 65000, "income"] = np.where(
    np.random.rand(sum(df["income"] > 65000)) < 0.6, np.nan, df.loc[df["income"] > 65000, "income"]
)

# Overview
print(df.info())
print("\nMissing values:")
print(df.isnull().sum())
print(df.isnull().mean().round(3) * 100, "% missing")

# Missing pattern visualisation
import seaborn as sns
sns.heatmap(df.isnull(), cbar=False, yticklabels=False, cmap="viridis")
plt.title("Missing data pattern (yellow = missing)")
plt.tight_layout(), plt.show()


5.1.4. Simple Imputation Methods
Single imputation replaces each missing value with a single estimate:
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
import pandas as pd

# Mean imputation (suitable for MCAR numeric variables)
imp_mean = SimpleImputer(strategy="mean")
df["income_imputed"] = imp_mean.fit_transform(df[["income"]])

# Median imputation (robust to outliers)
imp_median = SimpleImputer(strategy="median")
df["income_med"] = imp_median.fit_transform(df[["income"]])

# Mode imputation (for categorical)
imp_mode = SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")
df["education_imputed"] = imp_mode.fit_transform(df[["education"]])

print(df[["income", "income_imputed", "income_med"]].describe())


5.1.5. Multiple Imputation
Multiple imputation creates several complete datasets (each with different plausible values), analyses each separately, and combines results using Rubin’s rules. It is the gold standard for MAR data.
from sklearn.experimental import enable_iterative_imputer
from sklearn.impute import IterativeImputer

# MICE (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations)
mice = IterativeImputer(max_iter=10, random_state=0)
df_imputed = pd.DataFrame(
    mice.fit_transform(df[["age", "income", "score"]]),
    columns=["age", "income_mice", "score"]
)

print(df_imputed.describe().round(1))


5.1.6. Handling Outliers
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

data = pd.Series([12, 14, 13, 15, 14, 16, 13, 100, 14, 15])  # 100 is an outlier

# IQR method
Q1, Q3 = data.quantile(0.25), data.quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
lower, upper = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR, Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

outliers = data[(data < lower) | (data > upper)]
clean    = data[(data >= lower) & (data <= upper)]

print(f"Outliers: {outliers.values}")
print(f"Clean mean:   {clean.mean():.2f}")
print(f"Original mean:{data.mean():.2f}")
Decision framework for outliers:

Verify — is it a data entry error or a genuine extreme value?
Document — record all decisions and their rationale
Handle — remove (if error), cap/winsorise, or transform (log scale); never silently discard



5.1.7. Data Quality Report
A systematic data quality report should cover:
def data_quality_report(df):
    report = pd.DataFrame({
        "dtype":        df.dtypes,
        "n_missing":    df.isnull().sum(),
        "pct_missing":  (df.isnull().mean() * 100).round(2),
        "n_unique":     df.nunique(),
        "min":          df.select_dtypes("number").min(),
        "max":          df.select_dtypes("number").max(),
        "mean":         df.select_dtypes("number").mean().round(2),
    })
    return report

print(data_quality_report(df))

title: 3.2. Data Visualization
text:
3.2.1. Why Visualisation Matters
A well-chosen plot communicates in seconds what a table of numbers takes minutes to interpret. Visualisation is not decoration — it is a core analytical tool:

Exploratory data analysis (EDA): spot distributions, outliers, and patterns before modelling
Communication: present results to non-technical stakeholders
Diagnosis: check model assumptions (residuals, Q-Q plots)
Quality control: identify data entry errors and anomalies


“The greatest value of a picture is when it forces us to notice what we never expected to see.” — John Tukey




3.2.2. Choosing the Right Chart







Goal
Chart type




Distribution of one variable
Histogram, density plot, box plot


Comparison across categories
Bar chart, dot plot


Relationship between two numeric variables
Scatter plot


Change over time
Line chart


Part-of-whole
Pie chart (use sparingly), stacked bar


Many variables at once
Heatmap, scatter matrix, parallel coordinates



Rule of thumb: if your chart requires a legend to decode more than 3–4 categories, consider splitting into facets.



3.2.3. Histograms and Density Plots
Histograms show the distribution of a numeric variable by counting observations in equal-width bins.
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(42)
df <- data.frame(income = rnorm(500, mean = 45000, sd = 12000))

# Histogram
ggplot(df, aes(x = income)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(title = "Income distribution",
       x = "Annual income (€)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

# Histogram + density curve
ggplot(df, aes(x = income)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_density(colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Income — histogram with density", x = "Annual income (€)") +
  theme_minimal()
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

np.random.seed(42)
income = np.random.normal(45000, 12000, 500)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].hist(income, bins=30, color="steelblue", edgecolor="white")
axes[0].set(title="Histogram", xlabel="Annual income (€)", ylabel="Count")

sns.histplot(income, kde=True, ax=axes[1], color="steelblue")
axes[1].set(title="Histogram + KDE", xlabel="Annual income (€)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



3.2.4. Box Plots
Box plots summarise the five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max) and highlight outliers.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

set.seed(1)
df <- data.frame(
  department = rep(c("Sales", "HR", "IT", "Finance"), each = 60),
  salary     = c(rnorm(60, 55000, 8000),
                 rnorm(60, 48000, 6000),
                 rnorm(60, 72000, 12000),
                 rnorm(60, 65000, 10000))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(department, salary, median), y = salary, fill = department)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE, outlier.colour = "red", outlier.shape = 1) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Salary distribution by department",
       x = NULL, y = "Annual salary (€)") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme_minimal()
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

departments = ["Sales"] * 60 + ["HR"] * 60 + ["IT"] * 60 + ["Finance"] * 60
salaries = (
    list(np.random.normal(55000, 8000, 60)) +
    list(np.random.normal(48000, 6000, 60)) +
    list(np.random.normal(72000, 12000, 60)) +
    list(np.random.normal(65000, 10000, 60))
)

import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({"Department": departments, "Salary": salaries})

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
order = df.groupby("Department")["Salary"].median().sort_values().index
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="Salary", y="Department", order=order, palette="Set2")
plt.title("Salary distribution by department")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



3.2.5. Bar Charts
Use bar charts for categorical comparisons of counts or aggregated values.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

sales <- data.frame(
  region  = c("North", "South", "East", "West", "Central"),
  revenue = c(420, 380, 510, 290, 460)
)

ggplot(sales, aes(x = reorder(region, -revenue), y = revenue, fill = region)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0("€", revenue, "k")),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = "Revenue by region (2023)", x = NULL, y = "Revenue (€000s)") +
  theme_minimal()






Tip



Always order bars by value (unless the categories have an inherent order). A random ordering forces the reader’s eye to hunt for patterns.





3.2.6. Scatter Plots
Scatter plots reveal the relationship between two numeric variables.
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(7)
n <- 150
df <- data.frame(
  ad_spend = runif(n, 10, 100),
  revenue  = 200 + 3.5 * runif(n, 10, 100) + rnorm(n, 0, 30),
  segment  = sample(c("SME", "Enterprise", "Startup"), n, replace = TRUE)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = ad_spend, y = revenue, colour = segment)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Ad spend vs. Revenue by segment",
       x = "Ad spend (€000s)", y = "Revenue (€000s)",
       colour = "Segment") +
  theme_minimal()
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(7)
n = 150
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "ad_spend": np.random.uniform(10, 100, n),
    "revenue":  200 + 3.5 * np.random.uniform(10, 100, n) + np.random.normal(0, 30, n),
    "segment":  np.random.choice(["SME", "Enterprise", "Startup"], n)
})

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="ad_spend", y="revenue",
                hue="segment", alpha=0.7)
sns.regplot(data=df, x="ad_spend", y="revenue",
            scatter=False, color="grey", line_kws={"linewidth": 1.5})
plt.title("Ad spend vs. Revenue")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



3.2.7. Line Charts (Time Series)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

set.seed(3)
ts_data <- data.frame(
  date    = seq(as.Date("2022-01-01"), as.Date("2023-12-01"), by = "month"),
  revenue = cumsum(rnorm(24, mean = 5, sd = 3)) + 100
)

ggplot(ts_data, aes(x = date, y = revenue)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Monthly revenue 2022–2023",
       x = NULL, y = "Revenue (€000s)") +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b %Y", date_breaks = "3 months") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))



3.2.8. Faceting — Small Multiples
When comparing multiple subgroups, facets (small multiples) are often cleaner than overlaying on one plot.
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(5)
df <- data.frame(
  year   = rep(2019:2023, each = 50),
  profit = c(rnorm(50, 100, 20), rnorm(50, 105, 22),
             rnorm(50, 98,  25), rnorm(50, 115, 18),
             rnorm(50, 130, 20))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = profit)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~year, nrow = 1) +
  labs(title = "Profit distribution by year", x = "Profit (€000s)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()



3.2.9. ggplot2 Grammar of Graphics
Every ggplot2 plot is built from layers:
ggplot(data = <DATA>,              # 1. data frame
       aes(x = <VAR>, y = <VAR>,   # 2. aesthetics: what maps to what
           colour = <VAR>,
           fill   = <VAR>)) +
  geom_<TYPE>() +                  # 3. geometric object (bar, point, line, ...)
  stat_<FUN>() +                   # 4. statistical transformation (optional)
  scale_<AES>_<TYPE>() +           # 5. axis/colour scales
  facet_wrap(~<VAR>) +             # 6. faceting (optional)
  labs(title = "...",              # 7. labels
       x = "...", y = "...") +
  theme_minimal()                  # 8. theme (appearance)
Common geoms:



Geom
Chart type




geom_point()
Scatter plot


geom_line()
Line chart


geom_col() / geom_bar()
Bar chart


geom_histogram()
Histogram


geom_density()
Density curve


geom_boxplot()
Box plot


geom_violin()
Violin plot


geom_tile()
Heatmap


geom_smooth()
Trend line (with CI)






3.2.10. Visualisation Best Practices
Do: - Use clear, descriptive titles and axis labels with units - Order categorical axes meaningfully (by value, not alphabetically) - Use colourblind-friendly palettes (viridis, ColorBrewer) - Show uncertainty (confidence intervals, error bars) - Keep it simple — remove chart junk
Avoid: - 3D charts (they distort perception of area) - Pie charts with more than 4 slices - Dual y-axes (they mislead about relationships) - Truncated y-axes that exaggerate differences - Colour for decoration rather than information
# Colourblind-friendly palette
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = department, y = salary, fill = department)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   # or "viridis"
  theme_minimal()

title: 3.1. Statistical Hypothesis Testing
text:
3.1.1. What Is Hypothesis Testing?
Statistical hypothesis testing is a formal procedure for deciding whether evidence from sample data is strong enough to support a claim about a population. It is a form of statistical inference — drawing conclusions about a population from a sample.
The procedure has five steps:

Formulate the null hypothesis (H₀) and the alternative hypothesis (H₁)
Choose a significance level α (typically 0.05)
Collect data and compute the test statistic
Compute the p-value (probability of observing the data if H₀ is true)
Decide: reject H₀ if p < α; fail to reject otherwise



3.1.2. Null and Alternative Hypotheses
The null hypothesis (H₀) is the default assumption — usually that there is no effect, no difference, or no relationship. The alternative hypothesis (H₁) is what we want to prove.
Examples:








Research question
H₀
H₁




Does the new product design increase conversion?
μ₁ = μ₂
μ₁ ≠ μ₂


Is the average order value different from €50?
μ = 50
μ ≠ 50


Does training improve performance?
μ_before = μ_after
μ_before < μ_after









WarningWhat “fail to reject H₀” means



We never accept the null hypothesis. Failing to reject H₀ simply means we did not find sufficient evidence against it — the data are consistent with H₀, but that does not prove H₀ is true.




3.1.3. The p-Value
The p-value is the probability of observing data at least as extreme as what we actually observed, assuming H₀ is true. It is not the probability that H₀ is true.

p < 0.05 → reject H₀ (result is “statistically significant” at the 5% level)
p ≥ 0.05 → do not reject H₀

A small p-value means the observed data would be very unlikely if H₀ were true, providing evidence against H₀.


3.1.4. Type I and Type II Errors








Decision
H₀ true
H₀ false




Reject H₀
Type I error (false positive) — probability = α
Correct (power = 1 − β)


Do not reject
Correct (probability = 1 − α)
Type II error (false negative) — probability = β




α (significance level) — the maximum acceptable probability of a Type I error
β — probability of a Type II error; statistical power = 1 − β
Reducing α increases β; the tradeoff is managed through sample size



3.1.5. Sampling and Sample Distributions
Population — the entire group of interest (all customers, all employees, all transactions). Sample — a subset of the population used to draw inferences.
The sampling distribution of a statistic (e.g., the mean) describes how that statistic varies across all possible samples of a given size. This distribution is the theoretical foundation for all hypothesis tests.

Sampling Methods








Method
Description
When to use




Simple random
Each unit has equal probability of selection
When sampling frame is known


Systematic
Every k-th element from a list
Convenient for large ordered lists


Stratified
Random sample within each stratum (group)
Ensures representation of subgroups


Cluster
Randomly select entire clusters
Cost-effective for geographically spread populations


Purposive
Non-random; expert judgement
Qualitative research; when random sampling is impossible





Sampling in Python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
population = pd.DataFrame({
    "customer_id": range(1, 10001),
    "age": np.random.randint(18, 70, 10000),
    "spend": np.random.exponential(scale=150, size=10000)
})

# Simple random sample (n=200)
sample_srs = population.sample(n=200, random_state=42)
print(f"Population mean spend: {population['spend'].mean():.2f}")
print(f"Sample mean spend:     {sample_srs['spend'].mean():.2f}")



3.1.6. Sampling Bias
A sampling method is biased if it systematically favours some outcomes over others.
Common sources of sampling bias:

Self-selection / voluntary response bias — participants choose whether to respond (online surveys, product reviews)
Pre-screening bias — a strict selection process creates a homogeneous sample
Representation bias — certain population groups are systematically excluded
Exclusion bias — deliberately or accidentally omitting a subgroup from analysis







Note



AI and sampling bias: Algorithms trained on biased data reproduce and often amplify that bias. Amazon’s recruiting tool (discontinued in 2018) downgraded resumes from women because historical hiring data reflected past discrimination — a textbook case of how exclusion bias propagates from data to model to decision.




3.1.7. Practical Example: One-Sample z-Test
A manufacturer claims that average battery life is 10 hours. A quality tester draws a sample of 50 batteries.
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(0)
# Simulated sample (true mean slightly below claim)
sample = np.random.normal(loc=9.7, scale=1.2, size=50)

# One-sample t-test (H0: mu = 10)
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_1samp(sample, popmean=10)

print(f"Sample mean:  {sample.mean():.3f}")
print(f"t-statistic:  {t_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value:      {p_value:.4f}")

if p_value < 0.05:
    print("Reject H0 — battery life is significantly different from 10h")
else:
    print("Fail to reject H0 — no significant difference from 10h")

title: 3.2. Central Limit Theorem
text:
3.2.1. The Core Idea
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is one of the most profound results in probability and statistics. It states:

If we draw a large number of independent random samples from any population with a finite mean μ and standard deviation σ, the distribution of sample means will be approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the original population distribution.

More precisely: for samples of size \(n\), the sample mean \(\bar{X}\) is approximately:
\[\bar{X} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)\]
The standard deviation of this sampling distribution is called the standard error:
\[SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]


3.2.2. Why It Matters
The CLT is the theoretical foundation for most frequentist hypothesis tests and confidence intervals. It explains why tools like the z-test and t-test produce valid results even when the underlying population is not normally distributed — as long as the sample is large enough (typically n ≥ 30 is used as a rough rule).
It also explains the law of large numbers: as sample size increases, the sample mean converges to the true population mean.


3.2.3. Demonstration in Python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(0)

# Population: highly non-normal (uniform distribution between 2 and 6)
population = np.random.uniform(low=2, high=6, size=100_000)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 4))

# Left: population distribution
axes[0].hist(population, bins=50, color="salmon", edgecolor="white")
axes[0].set_title("Population (uniform)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Value")

# Middle and right: sampling distributions for different n
for ax, n, color in zip(axes[1:], [10, 100], ["steelblue", "seagreen"]):
    sample_means = [np.mean(np.random.choice(population, size=n, replace=True))
                    for _ in range(5_000)]
    ax.hist(sample_means, bins=50, color=color, edgecolor="white")
    ax.set_title(f"Sample means (n = {n})")
    ax.set_xlabel("Sample mean")
    ax.axvline(np.mean(population), color="red", linestyle="--", label=f"μ = {np.mean(population):.2f}")
    ax.legend()

plt.suptitle("Central Limit Theorem: Non-normal population → Normal sampling distribution")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Observation: Even though the population is perfectly flat (uniform), the distribution of sample means becomes bell-shaped for n = 10 and nearly perfectly normal for n = 100.


3.2.4. Standard Error and Sample Size
The standard error \(SE = \sigma / \sqrt{n}\) has a crucial implication: doubling the sample size halves the standard error (uncertainty about the mean). To halve SE again you need to quadruple n — diminishing returns at large sample sizes.
import numpy as np

sigma = 10  # population SD
ns = [10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000]

print(f"{'n':>6}  {'SE':>8}")
for n in ns:
    se = sigma / np.sqrt(n)
    print(f"{n:>6}  {se:>8.3f}")


3.2.5. Confidence Intervals
The CLT lets us construct confidence intervals — ranges that contain the true population mean with a specified probability (e.g., 95%).
\[\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]
where \(z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96\) for a 95% CI.
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

np.random.seed(7)
sample = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=12, size=80)   # 80 customer satisfaction scores

n    = len(sample)
mean = sample.mean()
se   = sample.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n)
ci   = stats.t.interval(0.95, df=n-1, loc=mean, scale=se)

print(f"Sample mean:      {mean:.2f}")
print(f"Standard error:   {se:.3f}")
print(f"95% CI:           ({ci[0]:.2f}, {ci[1]:.2f})")


3.2.6. Practical Implications







Concept
Business use




SE decreases with n
Larger surveys give narrower confidence intervals — more precise estimates


CLT justifies z/t tests
We can test hypotheses about means without knowing the population distribution


CI vs. p-value
CIs communicate magnitude of uncertainty, not just significance — more informative for decision-making


Law of large numbers
Averages over many transactions (e.g., insurance, casino) are highly predictable even when individual outcomes are not









Note



Bootstrap: a modern alternative
When n is small or the statistic is complex (median, correlation), the CLT may not apply. Bootstrapping resamples the data with replacement thousands of times to empirically construct the sampling distribution — no distributional assumptions required.

title: 3.3. Chi-Square Test and t-Tests
text:
Part A: Chi-Square Test (\(\chi^2\))
Two of the most frequently used statistical tests in business research are the chi-square test (for categorical data) and the t-test (for comparing means of continuous data).
crumbs: 11  3.3. Chi-Square Test and t-Tests

title: 3.3. Chi-Square Test and t-Tests
text:
3.3.1. What Is the Chi-Square Test?
Pearson’s chi-square test is a non-parametric test for categorical variables. It tests whether observed frequencies are significantly different from expected frequencies.
There are two variants:







Test
Purpose




Goodness of fit
Does one categorical variable follow an expected distribution?


Test of independence
Are two categorical variables related to each other?



Both use the same formula:
\[\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}\]
where \(O\) is the observed frequency and \(E\) is the expected frequency under H₀.


3.3.2. Chi-Square Test of Independence
Example: A retail chain wants to know whether customer gender is associated with product category preference.
H₀: Gender and product preference are independent. H₁: There is an association between gender and preference.
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency
import pandas as pd

# Observed contingency table
observed = np.array([
    [120, 80, 50],   # Female: Electronics, Clothing, Food
    [90,  40, 70],   # Male:   Electronics, Clothing, Food
])

chi2, p, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(observed)

print(f"Chi-square statistic: {chi2:.3f}")
print(f"Degrees of freedom:   {dof}")
print(f"p-value:              {p:.4f}")

if p < 0.05:
    print("Reject H0 — there IS an association between gender and product preference")
else:
    print("Fail to reject H0 — no significant association")


3.3.3. Goodness of Fit
Example: A marketing team expects customers to visit equally across five weekdays. Actual visit counts differ — is this due to chance?
from scipy.stats import chisquare

observed = [95, 112, 88, 130, 75]   # Mon–Fri visits
expected = [100, 100, 100, 100, 100]  # uniform expectation

chi2, p = chisquare(f_obs=observed, f_exp=expected)
print(f"chi2 = {chi2:.3f}, p = {p:.4f}")


3.3.4. Assumptions

Observations are independent
Each cell expected frequency ≥ 5 (use Fisher’s exact test for small samples)
Data must be counts (not percentages or means)
crumbs: 11  3.3. Chi-Square Test and t-Tests

title: 3.3. Chi-Square Test and t-Tests
text:
Part B: Student’s t-Test

3.3.5. What Is the t-Test?
The t-test compares the means of continuous variables and determines whether observed differences are statistically significant. It is a parametric test, assuming normality (or n large enough for CLT to apply) and independence.
Three types:



Type
When to use




One-sample
Compare a sample mean to a known/hypothesised value


Independent two-sample
Compare means of two separate groups


Paired
Compare means before and after (same subjects)





3.3.6. One-Sample t-Test
Example: The standard delivery time is 5 days. A sample of 30 deliveries yields a mean of 5.8 days. Is this significantly different?
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(1)
deliveries = np.random.normal(loc=5.8, scale=1.5, size=30)

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_1samp(deliveries, popmean=5.0)

print(f"Sample mean:  {deliveries.mean():.2f} days")
print(f"t = {t_stat:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}")


3.3.7. Independent Two-Sample t-Test
Example: Does the new website layout (Group B) produce higher average session times than the old layout (Group A)?
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
group_A = np.random.normal(loc=3.2, scale=0.8, size=50)  # Old layout
group_B = np.random.normal(loc=3.7, scale=0.9, size=50)  # New layout

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(group_A, group_B, equal_var=False)  # Welch's t-test

print(f"Mean A: {group_A.mean():.2f} min  |  Mean B: {group_B.mean():.2f} min")
print(f"t = {t_stat:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}")

if p_value < 0.05:
    print("Reject H0 — the new layout significantly increases session time")


3.3.8. Paired t-Test
Example: Do employees perform better after a training programme?
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(10)
before = np.random.normal(loc=65, scale=10, size=25)
after  = before + np.random.normal(loc=8, scale=5, size=25)  # improvement

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_rel(before, after)

print(f"Mean before: {before.mean():.1f}  |  Mean after: {after.mean():.1f}")
print(f"Mean difference: {(after - before).mean():.1f}")
print(f"t = {t_stat:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}")


3.3.9. Assumptions and When to Use Non-Parametric Alternatives








Assumption
t-Test
Violated → use




Normality (or large n)
Required
Wilcoxon signed-rank (paired), Mann-Whitney U (independent)


Independence
Required
Paired t-test if same subjects


Equal variances
For standard 2-sample
Use Welch’s t-test (equal_var=False)





3.3.10. One-Tailed vs. Two-Tailed Tests

Two-tailed (H₁: μ₁ ≠ μ₂): testing for any difference, regardless of direction
One-tailed (H₁: μ₁ > μ₂ or μ₁ < μ₂): testing for a difference in a specific direction

In practice, two-tailed tests are preferred unless there is a strong a priori theoretical reason to test in one direction only — one-tailed tests are easier to “pass” and are prone to misuse.
# Two-tailed (default)
t, p_two = stats.ttest_ind(group_A, group_B, equal_var=False)

# One-tailed (testing if B > A)
p_one = p_two / 2 if t < 0 else 1 - p_two / 2

print(f"Two-tailed p: {p_two:.4f}")
print(f"One-tailed p (B > A): {p_one:.4f}")
